### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

#### Argument for Incremental Change

- Deepfakes are an extension/evolution of existing technologies like photoshop, video editing etc. $\blacksquare$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

A well-balanced response considering both perspectives is expected. Credit should be given for well-substantiated examples and arguments from either side.

### Source A: Killer Robots: New UN Report Urges Treaty by 2026
The UN secretary-general has called for states to conclude a new international treaty by 2026 to prohibit weapons systems without human control or oversight and that cannot be used in compliance with international humanitarian law. The treaty should regulate all types of autonomous weapons systems. The report reflects 58 submissions from over 73 countries and 33 submissions from the International Committee of the Red Cross and civil society groups. The UN General Assembly is considered a venue for inclusive discussions on autonomous weapons systems, considering international peace and security concerns.

### Source B: Operation PEACE STORM: The Role of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Autonomous Weapons in Libya’s Offensive Strategy
On 27 March 2020, the Prime Minister of Libya, Faiez Serraj, announced the commencement of Operation PEACE STORM, which moved GNA-AF to the offensive along the coastal littoral. The combination of the Gabya-class frigates and Korkut short-range air defence systems provided a capability to place a mobile air defence bubble around GNA-AF ground units, which took Hafter Affiliated Forces (HAF) air assets out of the military equation. Libya classifies HAF as a terrorist rebel organization. The enhanced operational intelligence capability included Turkish-operated signal intelligence and the intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance provided by Bayraktar TB-2 and probably TAI Anka S unmanned combat aerial vehicles. This allowed for the development of an asymmetrical war of attrition designed to degrade HAF ground unit capability. The GNA-AF breakout of Tripoli was supported with Firtina T155 155 mm self-propelled guns and T-122 Sakarya multi-launch rocket systems firing extended range precision munitions against the mid-twentieth century main battle tanks and heavy artillery used by HAF. 

Logistics convoys and retreating HAF were subsequently hunted down and remotely engaged by the unmanned combat aerial vehicles or the lethal autonomous weapons systems such as the STM Kargu-2 (see annex 30) and other loitering munitions. The lethal autonomous weapons systems were programmed to attack targets without requiring data connectivity between the operator and the munition: in effect, a true ‘fire, forget and find’ capability. The unmanned combat aerial vehicles and the small drone intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance capability of HAF were neutralized by electronic jamming from the Koral electronic warfare system.

### Source C: The Story of America’s Very first Drone Strike 
In the autumn of 2001, however, the United States was unwilling to launch a full-scale land invasion in a region 7000 miles from home. Instead, a plan evolved to send into Afghanistan a small number of CIA agents and Special Forces in support of anti-Taliban militias, with the aid of the US Air Force. That first October night was a powerful display of coordination involving laser- guided munitions dropped from the air and Tomahawk cruise missiles launched from the sea. General Tommy Franks, who then led the US Central Command (CENTCOM), the military command overseeing operations in Afghanistan, wrote in his memoir American Soldier that the assault involved in total some 40,000 personnel, 393 aircraft, and 32 ships. But one aircraft did not feature at all in the Air Force’s complex planning: a tiny, CIA-controlled, propeller-driven spy drone, Predator tailfin number 3034 which had crept into Afghanistan some hours earlier. It now hangs suspended in the Smithsonian Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., its place in history assured. Yet its actions that first night of the war – in which numerous agencies in the vast US military-intelligence machine each played sharply contradictory roles – remain steeped in controversy. 

### Source D 
Human Rights Watch released a report stating that representatives from around 50 countries will meet in the summer of 2021 at the UN to discuss worldwide policy alignment on ‘killer robots’ or ‘lethal autonomous weapons systems’. In their report, Human Rights Watch expressed objections to delegating lethal force to machines without the presence of meaningful human control. Bonnie Docherty, senior arms research at Human Rights Watch said: ‘The fundamental moral, legal and security concerns raised by autonomous weapons systems warrant a strong and urgent response in the form of a new international treaty ... International law needs to be expanded to create new rules that ensure human control and accountability in the use of force.’ Human Rights Watch proposes a treaty that covers the use of all weapons that operate autonomously that includes limitations and restrictions such as banning the use of killer robots, with many claims reinforcing that meaningful human control must be involved in the selection and engagement of targets. It goes on to define the scope and prevalence of ‘meaningful human control’ to ensure that humans have access to the data, risks and potential impacts prior to authorizing an attack.

### The Role of Firewalls in Network Security
Firewalls are critical for network security, acting as barriers between internal networks and external threats. They control incoming and outgoing traffic, protecting against unauthorized access and cyber attacks. However, configuring firewalls effectively can be challenging, especially in large organizations.

parts.content: Evaluate the role of firewalls in securing organizational networks, considering their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation.
parts.markscheme:
Firewalls play a crucial role in securing organizational networks by acting as a barrier between trusted and untrusted networks, controlling the flow of traffic based on predefined security rules. Their effectiveness and potential challenges in implementation can be evaluated as follows:

- Firewalls provide a first line of defense against unauthorized access and malicious attacks by:
  - Filtering incoming and outgoing network traffic based on IP addresses, ports, and protocols <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Blocking known malicious traffic and preventing unauthorized access to internal resources <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Enforcing security policies and access control lists (ACLs) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Effectiveness of firewalls:
  - They can effectively prevent many types of network-based attacks, such as distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks, port scans, and unauthorized access attempts <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls can log and monitor network traffic, providing valuable information for security analysis and incident response <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - They can be configured to allow or deny specific types of traffic, providing granular control over network access <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Potential challenges in implementation:
  - Configuring and maintaining firewalls can be complex, especially in large organizations with diverse network architectures and security requirements <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Firewalls may not be effective against advanced threats, such as zero-day attacks, encrypted traffic, or insider threats <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Improper configuration or rule management can lead to security vulnerabilities or unintended network disruptions <marks>1 mark</marks>
  <commonMistake>Relying solely on firewalls as the primary security measure, neglecting other security controls like intrusion detection/prevention systems, antivirus software, and user awareness training</commonMistake> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<examTip>Discuss the importance of regularly updating firewall rules and software to address emerging threats and vulnerabilities</examTip> <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Candidates should demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of firewalls, their capabilities, limitations, and the need for a multi-layered security approach</note>

parts.content:
**Cameras in school**

The principal at Flynn School has received requests from parents saying that they would like to monitor their children’s performance in school more closely. He is considering extending the school’s IT system by installing cameras linked to facial recognition software that can record student behaviour in lessons.

The facial recognition software can determine a student’s attention level and behaviour, such as identifying if they are listening, answering questions, talking with other students, or sleeping. The software uses machine learning to analyse each student’s behaviour and gives them a weekly score that is automatically emailed to their parents.

The principal claims that monitoring students’ behaviour more closely will improve the teaching and learning that takes place.

Discuss whether Flynn School should introduce a facial recognition system that uses machine learning to analyse each student’s behaviour and give them a score that is automatically emailed to their parents.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

**Advantages** **of implementing the facial recognition system:**

*   May provide reassurance to students and parents about their safety while at school (values).
*   May provide useful information to the school that could be used to feedback into possible teaching and learning strategies (values).
*   May provide additional supporting evidence/data if there is a difference of opinions (values).
*   May reduce the need for teachers to have to register students at the start of lessons or the beginning/end of each day, ie freeing up staff time (systems).
*   May be able to create attendance/lateness reports if necessary (systems).
*   May motivate students to work harder, participate frequently (values).

**Disadvantages** **of implementing the facial recognition system:**

*   May be seen as intrusive by students and teachers (ethics, values, surveillance).
*   May be used as part of the management of teachers (ethics, values, surveillance).
*   May not pick up the nuances in student and teacher interaction, for example, the context of the behaviour may be important, and this will not be recorded by the facial recognition system (systems, algorithms)
*   Can teachers and students “game” the system, so the teaching and learning experience may be geared to satisfy the algorithms used in the facial recognition software (values, surveillance).
*   Cost of implementation – hardware, software.

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** education, acceptability, cost, anonymity, surveillance, privacy, algorithms, change, power, systems, values, ethics

_

parts.content: Evaluate the challenges of implementing algorithmic transparency and accountability in criminal justice, particularly with “black box” algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Key Points

- Algorithmic transparency and accountability are important for ensuring fairness and due process in criminal justice systems
- "Black box" algorithms lack transparency, making it difficult to understand how decisions are made
- This can lead to biased or discriminatory outcomes, violating principles of justice

### Possible Arguments

- Lack of transparency means defendants/public cannot scrutinize algorithms for bias or errors <marks>1</marks>
- Opaque decision-making processes undermine due process and right to fair trial <marks>1</marks>
- Algorithms may perpetuate historical biases in data against minority groups <marks>1</marks>
- Proprietary algorithms used by private companies are not open to public audit <marks>1</marks>
- Difficulty in assigning accountability when errors/biases occur in complex systems <marks>1</marks>

### Counterarguments

- Algorithms can be more objective/consistent than human decision-making <marks>1</marks>
- Some privacy/security is needed to prevent gaming of algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Transparency could reveal sensitive information about individuals <marks>1</marks>
- Auditing/testing can validate algorithms without full transparency <marks>1</marks>

### Potential Solutions

- Regulatory oversight and auditing requirements for algorithms <marks>1</marks>
- Open-sourcing algorithms for public scrutiny and accountability <marks>1</marks>
- Human oversight/ability to override algorithmic decisions <marks>1</marks>
- Ensuring representative and unbiased training data <marks>1</marks>

<note>Credit all relevant points from both sides. Maximum 12 marks.</note>

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Importance of Data Storage and Memory in Computers
Data storage (like hard drives and SSDs) holds files permanently, while memory (RAM) stores temporary data, supporting the computer’s processing needs. Effective storage and memory are critical to speed and multitasking abilities in devices from smartphones to mainframes.

For example, an SSD in a laptop allows fast data retrieval, boosting performance for tasks like video editing and gaming.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/719c25e9-ea0c-4aa4-98b6-94111f69cfdc)

### Source B: Wearable Devices: Integrating Computers into Daily Life
Wearable computers, such as smartwatches and fitness trackers, integrate technology into daily life, providing health monitoring, communication, and real-time data tracking. These devices are lightweight and adaptable, making them practical for continuous use.

For instance, a smartwatch can monitor heart rate, track physical activity, and send alerts, helping users maintain their health goals.


### Source C: The Impact of Moore’s Law on Technology Progression
Moore’s Law states that the number of transistors in a microchip doubles approximately every two years, boosting processing power. This principle has driven computing advancements, leading to smaller, faster, and more efficient devices.

For example, Moore’s Law facilitated the evolution from bulky desktop computers to powerful smartphones that fit in a pocket.


### Source D: Haptic and Graphical User Interfaces in Computing
User interfaces (UIs) allow people to interact with computers. Graphical user interfaces (GUIs) use icons and visuals to make navigation intuitive, while haptic interfaces provide touch feedback, enhancing the tactile experience in applications like gaming and virtual reality.

For example, smartphones combine haptic feedback with touchscreens, making interactions more intuitive for users.

parts.markscheme:
### Introduction

Technological advancements in computing have significantly influenced user experience and lifestyle by enhancing accessibility, productivity, and connectivity. Advancements in storage, processing, interfaces, and wearable technology have played a crucial role in this transformation.

### Enhanced User Experience Through Storage and Memory (Source A)

- SSD Storage: Solid-state drives (SSDs) allow faster data retrieval, supporting performance-heavy tasks like video editing, gaming, and multitasking on personal computers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- RAM: Increased random-access memory (RAM) improves multitasking capabilities, making interactions with multiple apps smooth and efficient, enhancing both professional and personal productivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Wearable Devices and Lifestyle Integration (Source B)

- Health Tracking: Wearable devices like fitness trackers and smartwatches support health management, making it easier for users to monitor metrics such as activity levels and sleep patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Convenient Connectivity: Real-time alerts and notifications integrate digital interactions seamlessly into daily routines, allowing users to stay connected and informed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Progression of Device Capability (Moore's Law - Source C)

- Device Miniaturization: Moore's Law has led to smaller, more powerful devices, from desktop computers to smartphones, making computing power portable and accessible. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Power Efficiency: Increasing power within smaller devices allows for high-performance applications on-the-go, influencing work habits and enhancing flexibility in computing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Should we completely automate journalism?**

Some of the news articles that you read are written by automated journalism software. This software uses algorithms and natural language generators to turn facts and trends into news stories.

_Narrative Science_, a company that produces automated journalism software, predicts that by 2026 up to 90 % of news articles could be generated by machine learning algorithms.

Discuss whether it is acceptable for news articles to be generated by automated journalism software.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why automated journalism software is acceptable (claim):**

*   Automation leads to articles being generated faster – this is important for breaking news (systems).
*   It allows papers to reach a lot of niche audiences therefore increasing its reach.
*   It can give reporters more time to do in-depth analysis of the articles they write.
*   It can free up reporters for stories that require human thought / can eliminate some of the more mundane/boring jobs and allow reporters to focus on more thought-provoking stories.
*   It cannot deal with subjective material, so reporters’ jobs are not at risk (values).

**Reasons why automated journalism software is not acceptable (counter-claim):**

*   It takes time and money to train the machine learning algorithms (systems).
*   Robotic journalism can provide statistics but not analysis.
*   Robotic journalism can’t provide the human perspective that makes articles interesting / may have inherent biases (values).
*   Robotic journalism can create and promulgate “fake news”, as they will not know whether a story is true or not.
*   Robotic journalism can’t discover news stories; they are restricted to information available online / the accuracy of the underlying data still matters.
*   Robotic journalism is designed by humans who make mistakes, so robots can make mistakes too. Who is accountable for these mistakes? What regulations are in force? Where does power lie?
*   Some newspapers do not disclose what they are doing (lack of transparency).
*   Data will have to be constantly checked to make sure it is up to date.
*   Journalists, especially inexperienced ones who are still learning. will lose jobs as a result of the automation of the process.

_  
Note to examiners – accept terms such as automated journalism_, _algorithmic journalism or robotic journalism._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

  
**Keywords:** economic, regulations, codes, policies, transparency, accountability, bias, perspective, automation, machine learning, algorithm, change, power, systems, values

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**  


This pre-released statement is provided to support you in planning extended inquiries into the societal impacts of digital systems on global well-being, particularly where work allocation and pay are shaped by platform rules.  


**ACCOMPANYING SOURCE BOOKLET**  


**Source 1**  


A gig platform, ShiftNow, uses an AI-driven system to match workers to short “shifts” for delivery, cleaning, and event staffing. Workers are shown shift offers with a time window to accept. ShiftNow states that ranking is based on “reliability and customer experience,” including acceptance rate, on-time arrival (tracked by GPS), completion rate, and average customer rating. Pay is calculated using a base rate plus modifiers such as distance, urgency, and “service quality adjustments.” The platform applies automatic penalties for late arrival and for cancellations within 2 hours of a shift start, and it may temporarily reduce visibility of future offers for workers with repeated issues.  

Workers can view an earnings dashboard after each shift, but the platform does not fully explain how each modifier is computed. ShiftNow offers an in-app dispute process: workers select a category (pay, rating, penalty, deactivation), upload evidence (screenshots, photos, messages), and receive a decision “typically within 7 days.” For some issues (such as deactivation after repeated cancellations), the platform states decisions are “final unless new evidence is provided.” Worker representatives have requested clearer timelines, human review for high-impact decisions, and an option to challenge customer ratings believed to be discriminatory or retaliatory.  




![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/233b507a-28be-4773-b1d1-0d7aa3d7ccc3)

**Answer points**

* Visual communication of earnings breakdown to workers after each shift <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how AI-driven matching, pay modifiers, and automated penalties could affect income security and worker protections in gig work.
parts.markscheme:
**Explain (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Pay modifiers such as distance, urgency, and service quality adjustments can make earnings unpredictable and volatile <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Lack of explanation of how modifiers are computed reduces worker ability to plan and budget effectively <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Short acceptance windows combined with acceptance rate ranking pressure workers to accept undesirable or unsafe shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Automated penalties for late arrival or cancellations within two hours can punish genuine constraints such as caregiving, transit issues, or illness <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Automatic penalties function as a form of discipline without due process or consideration of individual circumstances <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The dispute process requires workers to provide evidence and decisions take about seven days which delays resolution of income issues <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Decisions about deactivation being final unless new evidence is provided reduce procedural protections and avenues for appeal <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The combination of these factors increases income insecurity by making earnings unpredictable and subject to sudden deductions <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Worker protections are weakened because automated systems lack human judgement for exceptional circumstances and appeals are difficult to access <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Opaque algorithms and limited explanation prevent workers from understanding or challenging decisions that affect their income <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a mechanism such as matching, modifiers, or penalties.  
Award <marks>2 marks</marks> for explaining how that mechanism affects income security or worker protections with clear causal links.  
Repeat this pattern for up to three distinct mechanisms to reach the full <marks>6 marks</marks>.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses must explain the relationship between the system components and their effects rather than merely listing features. To earn full marks, answers must explicitly link the identified mechanisms to income security or worker protections. Partial credit can be awarded for identifying mechanisms without fully explaining their effects. Responses that focus only on one mechanism can earn a maximum of <marks>3 marks</marks>.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
AI driven matching and pay modifiers affect income security because earnings become volatile and unpredictable. When pay varies based on factors such as distance, urgency, and service quality, workers cannot reliably predict or plan their income. The lack of explanation for how these modifiers are computed further reduces their ability to budget and manage their finances.  

  
Automated penalties for late arrival or cancellations weaken worker protections by applying discipline without due process. A penalty for cancellation within two hours of a shift can punish workers for genuine constraints such as illness, caregiving responsibilities, or transit delays. The system does not consider individual circumstances, which means workers lose income and access to future shifts through no fault of their own.  

  
The dispute process further reduces protections because it places the burden of proof on workers. Workers must upload evidence and wait about seven days for a decision, which delays resolution of urgent income issues. When decisions about deactivation are final unless new evidence is provided, workers have limited avenues to challenge unfair outcomes. These factors combine to create income insecurity and undermine protections for gig workers.

parts.content: Evaluate the gig labour intervention in terms of innovation and acceptability.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* AI driven matching represents innovation because it automates the connection between workers and shifts <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Flexible short shifts provide workers with scheduling adaptability and choice in work hours <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Dynamic pay modifiers allow the platform to respond quickly to changing supply and demand conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Dashboard visibility of earnings after shifts provides partial transparency and accountability <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Opaque modifiers reduce acceptability because workers cannot understand how their pay is calculated <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Automatic penalties and decisions for deactivation reduce acceptability because they feel unfair and difficult to challenge <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Ratings systems may be perceived as unfair or discriminatory which undermines trust in the platform <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Worker representatives requesting human review and the ability to challenge ratings indicate concerns about fairness and accountability <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Acceptability varies across worker groups such as caregivers, newcomers, and migrants who have different availability constraints <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The platform demonstrates innovation through technology but faces acceptability challenges due to transparency and fairness concerns <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a valid point about innovation supported by reference to Source 1.  
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a valid point about acceptability balanced against innovation.  
Repeat this pattern up to a maximum of <marks>8 marks</marks>.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>The evaluation must consider both innovation and acceptability rather than focusing on only one aspect. Responses that only discuss innovation without addressing acceptability cannot earn full marks. Credit should be given for balanced judgements that acknowledge benefits and drawbacks of the gig labour intervention. References to worker representative requests and pre release themes can strengthen the evaluation.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
The gig labour intervention shows innovation through AI driven matching that automatically connects workers to shifts based on reliability and customer experience metrics. The use of flexible short shifts allows workers to choose when they work, and dynamic pay modifiers enable the platform to adjust quickly to factors such as distance and urgency. The provision of an earnings dashboard after each shift represents partial innovation in transparency, showing workers some information about their pay and performance.  

  
However, acceptability is reduced by several factors. Opaque pay modifiers mean workers cannot fully understand how their earnings are calculated, which creates frustration and distrust. Automatic penalties for late arrival or cancellation within two hours can punish workers for circumstances beyond their control, and decisions about deactivation being final unless new evidence is provided limit recourse. Ratings systems may be perceived as unfair or discriminatory, and worker representatives have requested human review for high impact decisions and the ability to challenge problematic ratings. Acceptability also varies across worker groups, as caregivers, newcomers, and migrants may face greater availability constraints that make the platform rules less equitable. Therefore, while the intervention demonstrates technological innovation, its acceptability is limited by transparency and fairness concerns.

parts.content: The government is evaluating whether to intervene in ShiftNow. On the assumption that they do, drawing on the source material regarding ShiftNow and your own inquiries, recommend a governance and oversight intervention for ShiftNow which will maximise worker protections and platform efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Implement transparency requirements by requiring platforms to explain pay modifier ranges, penalty logic, and ranking factors in plain language <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Provide itemised receipts for each shift showing how all pay components are calculated <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Establish due process standards including mandatory human review for deactivation and high impact penalties <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Set appeal timelines shorter than seven days for urgent pay issues to ensure timely resolution <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Create anti discrimination and rating governance systems that allow workers to contest discriminatory or retaliatory ratings <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Require platforms to audit rating disparities and take corrective action when unfair patterns are identified <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Implement penalty safeguards such as caps, grace periods, and documented exceptions for illness, safety, and emergencies <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Provide data access for regulators including aggregated metrics on earnings, penalties, and deactivations <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Mandate independent audits of platform algorithms and decision making systems to ensure fairness and compliance <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Require consultation with worker representatives in designing and reviewing platform policies <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Balance intervention costs such as increased staffing for review processes against benefits such as lower worker churn and higher trust <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Cite relevant laws, case studies, or policies that support the recommended intervention <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Explicitly weigh trade offs between maintaining platform efficiency and ensuring worker protections <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for recommending a specific governance or oversight intervention.  
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for explaining how the intervention maximises worker protections.  
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for explaining how the intervention maintains platform efficiency.  
Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for using Source 1 effectively and drawing on relevant own inquiries.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>The recommendation must specifically address governance and oversight rather than general advice to workers. Responses that do not use Source 1 to inform the recommendation cannot earn full marks. Own inquiries should cite specific laws, policies, or case studies rather than vague references. The answer must explicitly balance worker protections against platform efficiency. Responses that focus only on protections or only on efficiency cannot earn full marks.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
I recommend that the government implement a transparency and due process framework for gig platforms such as ShiftNow. This intervention should require platforms to explain pay modifier ranges, penalty logic, and ranking factors in clear language that workers can understand. Platforms must provide itemised receipts for each shift showing how base pay and all modifiers are calculated. This directly addresses the lack of transparency identified in Source 1 and helps workers predict and plan their income.  

  
The framework should establish due process standards including mandatory human review for deactivation and other high impact penalties. Appeal timelines should be shorter than seven days for urgent pay issues so that workers are not left without income for extended periods. These measures respond to worker representative concerns about automated decisions and limited recourse. Anti discrimination governance should allow workers to contest unfair ratings, supported by regular audits of rating disparities to identify systemic bias.  

  
Penalty safeguards such as caps, grace periods, and documented exceptions for illness, safety, and emergencies would prevent automated systems from punishing workers for genuine constraints. Regulators should receive aggregated data on earnings, penalties, and deactivations, and independent audits of algorithms should be mandated to ensure fairness and accountability.  

  
This recommendation draws on policies such as proposed algorithmic accountability laws and platform regulation initiatives that emphasise transparency and human oversight. While these measures increase administrative costs and may reduce some efficiency gains from automation, they can lower worker churn, increase trust, and reduce disputes. Overall, the intervention balances platform efficiency with stronger worker protections by ensuring that innovation does not come at the expense of fairness and due process.

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Black Box Problem: Algorithmic Transparency and Accountability
Many complex algorithms, particularly those used in machine learning, operate as ""black boxes,"" meaning their decision-making processes are not easily understood. This lack of transparency and accountability raises ethical concerns, especially when algorithms impact individuals' lives in areas like credit scoring or criminal justice.

For example, an algorithm used for credit scoring may make decisions without revealing the factors it considered, leading to a lack of accountability.


### Source B: Erosion of Human Judgment in Algorithmic Decision-Making
The rise of algorithmic decision-making can lead to a loss of human judgment, especially in areas where subjective understanding is crucial, like healthcare or social services. Over-reliance on algorithms may result in decisions that overlook nuanced human factors.

For instance, a diagnostic algorithm in healthcare might misinterpret symptoms that a human physician could recognize due to experience or intuition.


### Source C: Algorithm Efficiency: Measuring Time and Space Complexity
Algorithm efficiency is critical, especially in processing large datasets. Efficiency is measured by time complexity (how fast an algorithm runs) and space complexity (how much memory it uses). Balancing these aspects is essential in applications that require speed and performance, like data processing in finance.

For example, financial trading algorithms are optimized to make quick, efficient decisions based on market data to execute trades faster than human traders.


### Source D: Algorithmic Filtering and Prioritization in Social Media
Social media platforms use algorithms to filter and prioritize content, often shaping users' experiences and the information they see. These algorithms analyze user data to decide which posts appear first, influencing what trends, news, and advertisements are visible to each user.

For example, a social media algorithm might prioritize posts from close contacts and trending news topics to keep users engaged.

parts.content:
**The human face of student counselling**

Many Diploma Programme (DP) coordinators are finding they do not have enough time to interview every student before they choose their DP subjects. They are considering purchasing the Student Counselling System, a proprietary management information system (MIS).

The developers of the Student Counselling System claim that, if students complete online questionnaires to determine their interests, abilities and future goals, the system can give them very accurate results, resulting in less than 5% of students changing their diploma subjects. They also claim the Student Counselling System will allow DP coordinators to be able to interview every student, something they cannot do now.

Some DP coordinators have concerns about the automation of this process and believe that advising students is something that is best done by relying on their own professional judgement and intuition.

Discuss whether DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System rather than their own professional judgement and intuition when advising students about what DP subjects to study.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System (claim):**

*   If a large number of decisions based on a wide range of scenarios are programmed into the Student Counselling System, it should give accurate and reliable results (systems, feasibility).
*   Students may be able to carry out initial investigations themselves to determine whether their proposed choices are realistic (judgement).
*   If many of the scenarios are similar, the DP coordinator may be able to see a number of students together to give general advice, making the process more efficient.
*   Students may appreciate the consistency / transparency of the information provided by the Student Counselling System (equity).
*   In many cases, the scenario may be relatively straightforward, and the Student Counselling System may be able to provide consistent information that the DP coordinators would have in a fraction of the time, allowing them to focus on the students who do have atypical requirements (feasibility, costs).

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on their judgement (counter-claim):**

*   DP coordinators know that special circumstances / family issues / personal information can have an impact on the decision and this will require their professional judgement, intuition, valuesandethics.
*   They may encounter scenarios that are not part of the system, which limit its usability / feasibility.
*   DP coordinators might be aware of new subjects/majors that have not been updated into the system (change).
*   Students may have multiple interests, and the Student Counselling System cannot give accurate recommendations.
*   Giving career advice is too important to be left to the algorithms that exist within the Student Counselling System (values, ethics).

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:**automation, change, power, systems, values, ethics, judgement, intuition, consistency, transparency, efficiency, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, equity, costs, feasibility

parts.content:
**A cashless society**

In the near future, it is possible that cash will not be accepted as a means of payment in Sweden. People are already using alternative ways of paying, such as mobile payment, card payment and internet payment. Currently, over 95% of citizens in Sweden have internet access.

Many people in Sweden claim there are advantages of using an app developed by Swish. The Swish app allows friends to share a restaurant bill, pay where credit or debit cards are not accepted, for babysitting or parking tickets, or make a donation at church.

However, other people in Sweden claim that making the Swish app the only means of payment may increase inequalities within the country.

Discuss whether countries should pass legislation making apps such as Swish the only means of payment.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Benefits of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (claim)**

*   No need to carry cash (digitalization) / credit cards – no risk of being stolen/no risk of not having enough cash.
*   No risk of credit card being used fraudulently (values).
*   Transactions are recorded – there is proof of payment, and the system should increase transparency / acceptability.
*   Payments are made immediately – no need to wait until person has time to go to bank to get cash (feasibility).
*   Can solve other problems regarding money – bills can be shared, one person pays, and money is transferred (feasibility).
*   In an emergency, money can be transferred to dependents without them being close (e.g., children at university) (feasibility).
*   Allows money transactions between individuals.
*   Can limit the amount of money to be transferred, thus preventing individuals spending more than they have available (acceptability / feasibility).
*   Many people are already used to the app, so it would be a good choice if the country were going cashless (acceptability).
*   Easier for users to track budgets / spending, as all transactions are digital (transparency).

**Disadvantages of apps such as Swish _being_ the only form of payment (counter-claim)**

*   Swedish banks will be able to obtain more data on their users’ transaction habits (privacy concern, ethics, values).
*   Is not available to people who do not have a bank account, so a potential digital divide concern (equity).
*   May be problematic for tourists who may not have the app or who cannot link the purchase to their bank account (systems, equity).
*   Removes the anonymity of the payee in transactions – the app may store a user’s transaction history. This would include date, item, recipient of the money and cost of the item (values, ethics).
*   The bank controls (power) the maximum amount of money that can be transferred, which may limit a person’s spending and may not be appropriate in certain situations (acceptability).
*   It may not be technically possible to make the transition from a society that uses cash for transactions to one that does not (change, systems).
*   If a single app is used, it would give Swish an unfair monopoly over the technology (power, values, ethics).
*   Digital divide – smartphone ownership and use by mature adults.
*   If a person loses their phone, breaks it, or its battery runs out, they have no way to pay for anything (systems, equity).
*   Failure in / lack of phone network coverage could affect when and where people could use the app (systems, equity).
*   Failure of the system / technical issues / down time would prevent people from making transactions (systems, feasibility).

_Accept implicit and explicit references to apps such as the Swish app._

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** business, families, digital divide, access, inclusion, acceptability, feasibility, equity, digitalization, anonymity, privacy, change, power, systems, values, ethics

parts.content: Evaluate the limitations of rule-based AI in adapting to complex tasks compared to advanced AI systems.
parts.markscheme:
### Rule-based AI limitations

- Rule-based AI systems operate based on a predefined set of rules and logic, making them inflexible and unable to adapt to complex or dynamic situations that require reasoning beyond the programmed rules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They lack the ability to learn and improve from experience or data, unlike advanced AI systems that use machine learning algorithms to continuously enhance their performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Rule-based systems struggle with ambiguity, uncertainty, and exceptions that are common in complex real-world scenarios, as they rely on strict rules and cannot handle unexpected situations effectively. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They are limited in their ability to process and understand natural language, making it challenging to interact with humans in a natural and contextual manner, which is crucial for complex tasks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Advanced AI capabilities

- Advanced AI systems like IBM's Watson use machine learning techniques, such as deep learning and natural language processing, to analyze vast amounts of data and identify patterns, enabling them to handle complex tasks more effectively. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They can continuously learn and improve their performance by ingesting and processing new data, adapting to changing circumstances and refining their decision-making capabilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Advanced AI systems can handle ambiguity, uncertainty, and exceptions more effectively by leveraging their ability to reason and make inferences based on the data they have processed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They can engage in natural language interactions, understanding context and nuances, making them better suited for tasks that require human-like communication and decision-making, such as medical diagnosis or customer service. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**  


Many people remain unbanked or underbanked due to barriers such as lack of formal identification, insecure housing addresses, unpredictable incomes, limited mobility, low trust in institutions, and low digital skills. In rural areas and informal settlements, distances to bank branches and the costs of transport and waiting times can make financial services inaccessible. Exclusion can limit people’s ability to receive wages securely, save safely, access credit on fair terms, or withstand shocks such as illness or climate-related events. It can also increase reliance on cash, which may expose individuals to theft, coercion, or exploitative intermediaries.  

Digital financial services are often presented as a remedy: mobile-based accounts, digital payments, and app-based micro-savings tools can reduce transaction costs and enable faster transfers. However, digital inclusion is not only about having an app. Meaningful access depends on affordability of devices and data, usability for low literacy and multiple languages, availability of support channels, and protection from scams and identity theft. Additionally, opaque fees, unclear limits, and confusing account “tiers” can cause harm even when services are technically available. In contexts where algorithmic checks are used to verify identity or detect fraud, decisions may be hard to understand and challenge.  


**Accompanying source booklet**  


**Source 1**  


A fintech company, PayLink, has launched a digital banking app offering a “basic account” intended for unbanked adults. Onboarding requires a smartphone which has a camera. Users enter a phone number, create a PIN, and complete identity checks using a photo of an ID document (if available) and a selfie. Users without formal ID can open a “limited account” with lower transaction limits after providing an alternative verification through a community partner (for example, a letter from a local organization plus a live photo at an agent location). The app supports two languages at launch and offers in-app chat support from 8am–6pm.  

Fees are described as: free peer-to-peer transfers within PayLink; a cash-out fee when withdrawing via agents; and a monthly “account maintenance” fee that is waived if a minimum balance is maintained. The company states it uses automated fraud detection to flag “unusual activity,” which may temporarily freeze transfers until users answer additional questions. PayLink says it provides “instant micro-loans” to some users after “responsible usage,” but the eligibility criteria are not explained in detail. The company has partnered with a mobile network operator to zero-rate access to the app, but users still need data for identity uploads if they onboard from outside an agent’s location.  


**Source 2**  


Excerpt from a financial regulator’s consumer advisory distributed through community organizations: “Providers must communicate fees, limits, and key risks in clear, prominent language before a customer confirms an account or transaction. Customers must have accessible complaint channels, including non-digital options. Where automated systems restrict access (for example freezing accounts), providers should offer timely review, explain the reason in plain language, and avoid discrimination against groups that may share devices, addresses, or phone numbers.”

parts.content: Analyse how PayLink’s design choices (for example onboarding steps, account tiers, and support options) may influence both adoption and user risk for unbanked populations.
parts.markscheme:
**Analyse (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Requiring a smartphone camera plus selfie and ID checks may reduce fraud but can reduce adoption among users without devices, data, or formal ID, creating a barrier for unbanked populations <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The limited account option via community partner verification can increase adoption for people lacking formal ID but lower transaction limits may limit the usefulness of the service and create a two tier system <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The maintenance fee that is waived with a minimum balance may incentivize saving for some users but can penalize low income users with unpredictable incomes, increasing the risk of unexpected charges <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* In app chat support available from 8am to 6pm may help adoption for users who can access it but limited hours and digital only support can increase risk when accounts are frozen or users face scams outside these hours <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Freezing transfers due to unusual activity can protect users against fraud but can also cause harm if explanations are unclear, review is slow, or shared devices or addresses are misread as suspicious <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Zero rating app access reduces data costs but users still need data for identity uploads if they onboard outside an agent location, which creates a remaining barrier <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> per analytical point that identifies a design choice and analyses its impact on adoption and user risk.  
Award a maximum of <marks>6 marks</marks>.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses must link specific design choices from Source 1 to both adoption and user risk. References to Source 2 or pre release concepts strengthen analysis. Full marks require evaluation of both positive and negative effects.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
PayLink requires a smartphone camera for selfie and ID checks, which may reduce fraud but also reduces adoption among unbanked users who lack devices, data, or formal ID.  

  
The limited account option through community partner verification increases adoption for users without ID but lower transaction limits reduce usefulness and create a two tier system that may feel exclusionary.  

  
The maintenance fee waiver based on minimum balances may incentivize saving but risks penalizing low income users with unpredictable incomes who face unexpected charges.  

  
In app chat support from 8am to 6pm helps adoption for supported users but limited hours and digital only support increase risk when accounts are frozen or users encounter scams outside these hours.  

  
Freezing transfers due to unusual activity protects against fraud but can cause harm through unclear explanations, slow reviews, or discrimination against users who share devices or addresses.

parts.content: Evaluate the digital banking intervention in terms of acceptability and ethics.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* The app supports only two languages, which may reduce acceptability in multilingual communities and limit usability for low literacy populations <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Perceived fairness of account tiers and fees affects user acceptability, and trust can be undermined by automatic freezes and unclear eligibility criteria for instant micro loans <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Availability of support and complaint channels affects acceptability, and Source 2 indicates expectations for non digital complaint options that PayLink does not clearly provide <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Consent and privacy concerns in collecting ID photos and selfies raise ethical issues, particularly for vulnerable populations who may not understand the implications of data collection <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Users without formal ID face exclusion risks, which creates ethical tensions between verification requirements and inclusion goals <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Automated restrictions such as account freezes must be explainable and reviewable, and Source 2 explicitly warns against discrimination against groups sharing devices, addresses, or phone numbers <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Fee transparency is an ethical concern, and Source 2 requires clear and prominent language before confirmation whereas Source 1 suggests potential complexity in fee structures <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The presence of automated fraud detection without transparent criteria raises ethical questions about accountability and the right to challenge algorithmic decisions <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for points addressing acceptability and up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for points addressing ethics.  
Award up to <marks>8 marks</marks> for evaluation that weighs strengths and limitations using Sources 1 and 2 and pre release concepts.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Evaluation requires considering both strengths and limitations. References to both sources and pre release statements are expected. Higher marks should show balanced judgement about acceptability and ethics rather than one sided criticism or praise.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
PayLink shows limited acceptability because it supports only two languages, which may not meet the needs of multilingual communities and users with low literacy. Perceived fairness affects acceptability, and users may lose trust due to automatic freezes and unclear eligibility for micro loans. Source 2 indicates that providers should offer non digital complaint channels, but PayLink relies on in app chat with limited hours, which may not meet acceptability standards.  

  
Ethically, collecting ID photos and selfies raises concerns about consent and privacy, especially for vulnerable users who may not understand data implications. Users without formal ID face exclusion risks, creating tension between verification needs and inclusion goals. Automated account freezes must be explainable and reviewable, and Source 2 warns against discrimination against users sharing devices or addresses. Fee transparency is another ethical issue, as Source 2 requires clear language before confirmation whereas Source 1 suggests fee complexity that may confuse users. The use of automated fraud detection without transparent criteria raises further questions about accountability and the right to challenge decisions.

parts.content: With reference to the launched app, the comments on the app, and your own inquiries, recommend a blended intervention ecosystem to improve financial inclusion and user protection around PayLink.
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

  
**Answer points**

  
* Agent or assisted onboarding pathways should be provided to help users without devices or digital skills <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Paper or alternative ID options should be available for users who lack formal identification documents <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Subsidized devices or shared safe onboarding points should be established to reduce access barriers <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Clear fee and limit screens should be displayed before users confirm accounts or transactions <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Scam education should be integrated into the app and provided through community partners <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Fraud freeze explanations should be provided in plain language with specific reasons for restrictions <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Rapid human review service level agreements should be implemented for addressing frozen accounts <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Hotline and in person complaint options should be available through community partners as required by Source 2 <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Independent audits for discrimination should be conducted to ensure fairness across user groups <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Regular reporting on freeze rates and outcomes should be published to enable accountability <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Compliance with regulator requirements should be maintained and demonstrated through transparency <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Language support beyond two languages should be expanded to improve accessibility <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Icons and audio prompts should be incorporated to support users with low literacy <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Community co design processes should be used to ensure services meet local needs and contexts <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for access pathway recommendations.  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for user protection recommendations.  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for complaint and governance recommendations.  
Award up to <marks>2 marks</marks> for design and inclusion recommendations.  
Award up to <marks>4 marks</marks> for integration of real examples, policies, or case studies and explicit trade off analysis.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Top band answers should cite or reference real examples, policies, or case studies and explicitly weigh trade offs such as fraud versus access, cost versus support coverage, and privacy versus verification. Recommendations must reference the launched app and consider both digital and non digital supports.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
A blended intervention ecosystem around PayLink should include agent or assisted onboarding pathways to help users without devices or digital skills access the service. Paper or alternative ID options should be available for users lacking formal identification, and subsidized devices or shared safe onboarding points should reduce access barriers in under served areas. User protection measures should include clear fee and limit screens before confirmation, integrated scam education through the app and community partners, and fraud freeze explanations in plain language. Rapid human review service level agreements should address frozen accounts quickly.  

  
Complaint channels must include hotline and in person options through community partners as required by Source 2. Governance should include independent audits for discrimination, regular reporting on freeze rates to enable accountability, and demonstrated compliance with regulator requirements. Design for inclusion should expand language support beyond two languages, incorporate icons and audio prompts for low literacy users, and use community co design processes to ensure services meet local needs and contexts. These measures balance fraud prevention against access, control costs while extending support coverage, and protect privacy while providing verification.

questions.specification:
In self-driving cars, algorithms must respond to various conditions like traffic lights, obstacles, and pedestrian movement. The components of these algorithms include variables (e.g., speed), conditionals (e.g., stop if obstacle detected), and loops (e.g., continuous monitoring of the road).

parts.content: Define ‘conditional’ in the context of an algorithm for self-driving cars.
parts.markscheme:
Define 'conditional' in the context of an algorithm for self-driving cars. <marks>2 marks</marks>

A conditional is a statement in the algorithm that executes actions based on specific criteria (e.g., if an obstacle is detected, the car stops).

$$
\text{if (obstacle detected):} \\
\quad \text{stop car}
$$

parts.content: Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms.
parts.markscheme:
### Identify two reasons why variables are critical for self-driving car algorithms. <marks>2 marks</marks>

1. Variables are used to store and manipulate data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) that is essential for the algorithm to make decisions.

2. Variables allow the algorithm to keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) and adjust its behavior accordingly.

<commonMistake>
Failing to recognize that variables are necessary to store and process dynamic data from the car's environment and internal state.
</commonMistake>

parts.content: Explain how loops improve efficiency in a self-driving car's algorithm for real-time monitoring.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>4 marks</marks>

Loops allow continuous monitoring of road conditions by repeatedly checking sensors, enabling quick responses to changes without repeating code manually.

$$
\text{while (car is running):} \\
\quad \text{check sensors} \\
\quad \text{update car state}
$$

parts.content: Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.
parts.markscheme:
### Discuss the challenges of creating self-driving algorithms without using variables or conditionals.

Without variables:
- It would be impossible to store and process data from sensors (e.g., distance to obstacles, speed, location) <marks>2 marks</marks>
- The algorithm could not keep track of the car's state (e.g., current speed, direction) or adjust its behavior accordingly <marks>2 marks</marks>

Without conditionals:
- The algorithm would lack the ability to make decisions based on specific criteria or situations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- It could not adapt its behavior based on changing road conditions or obstacles, leading to potential safety issues <marks>2 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
**PRE-RELEASED STATEMENT**  


Many communities are experiencing population aging, while service providers shift to “digital-first” models. Older adults are not a single group: they have different incomes, mobility needs, health conditions, languages, and experiences with technology. Barriers may include limited device access, unreliable connectivity, inaccessible design (small text, complex menus), and anxiety about scams or making mistakes. At the same time, older adults may be expected to manage more of their lives online, sometimes with reduced in-person alternatives.  

Digital inclusion initiatives can improve well-being by supporting independence and participation. However, they may also create new burdens: requiring travel to training sites, collecting personal data to “personalize” support, or relying on family caregivers who may not be available. How success is defined matters: counting attendance is not the same as ensuring people can complete meaningful tasks safely (e.g., booking appointments, accessing benefits, recognizing fraud). Local context also matters: rural/urban differences, disability access, cultural expectations, and trust in institutions.  


**Accompanying source booklet**  


**Source 1**  


A city-run “Digital Confidence for Seniors” programme is launching across community centres and libraries. It offers:  
* Small-group sessions (max 8 learners) twice a week for 6 weeks, plus optional drop-in help.  
* A choice of tracks: “Basics” (phone settings, messages, Wi‑Fi), “Everyday services” (appointments, transport, payments), and “Staying safe” (scam recognition, password habits).  
* Accessibility supports: large-print handouts, hearing-loop rooms, screen-reader compatible practice devices, and slower-paced instruction.  
* Staffing: one trainer, one volunteer assistant, and a rotating “cyber-safety advisor” from a local nonprofit.  
* Enrolment via phone, in person, or online; places prioritised for residents aged 70+ and those referred by health clinics.  
* Data collection: a sign-in sheet, optional pre/post confidence survey, and (with consent) a skills checklist noting tasks practiced.  


**Source 2**  


Excerpt from participant and caregiver feedback collected during a previous pilot:  

“I finally learned how to refill my prescription online, but the login codes arrive too fast and I panic.” (participant, 78)  

“The trainer was patient. I can video call my sister now.” (participant, 73)  

“My dad can’t travel easily. If it’s only at the library, he won’t go.” (caregiver)  

“I’m worried the programme makes people feel ashamed if they struggle.” (participant, 81)  

“We need more help spotting fake messages. That’s what scares people away.” (volunteer)  



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c0de4ad7-4c48-4e2c-ba44-6848c81ad2da)

parts.content: Identify two accessibility supports offered by the programme in Source 1.

**Identify (Total <marks>2 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

parts.content: Analyse how the programme’s enrolment and prioritisation approach in Source 1 could affect uneven access among older adults.
parts.markscheme:
**Analyse (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* Multiple enrolment channels such as phone, in person, and online increase access for people with low digital confidence and create more equitable entry points <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Prioritising people aged seventy and above may target those with higher needs but may exclude those in their sixties who also require support <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Relying on health clinic referrals may advantage those connected to clinics and disadvantage individuals who do not regularly use healthcare services <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Small group sizes and a six week schedule may limit overall capacity and create uneven access because only those who can commit to the time and travel can participate <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Delivery through community centres and libraries may support urban residents but may create unequal access for housebound individuals or those in rural areas <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The combination of prioritisation rules and capacity constraints can make access uneven if demand exceeds available places <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for analytical points that identify features of enrolment or prioritisation and explain how these create uneven access.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>To earn full marks, students must demonstrate analysis by linking programme features to uneven distribution of access. Descriptive answers should not be awarded the highest levels. Each analytical point is worth <marks>1 mark</marks>.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
The programme’s enrolment approach includes phone, in person, and online options, which can help people with limited digital skills enter the programme more easily. However, prioritising residents aged seventy or above may exclude people in their late sixties who still need support.  

  
Referral through clinics may favour people who already receive regular healthcare. Small group sizes and a six week commitment limit the number of places, which disadvantages older adults with unpredictable schedules or caregiving duties. Holding sessions only in community centres and libraries may also exclude housebound individuals who cannot travel. Together, these features can create uneven access because some groups are more likely than others to secure entry into the programme.

parts.content: Evaluate the intervention in terms of feasibility and innovation.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>8 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* The staffing model that includes a trainer, a volunteer, and a rotating cyber safety advisor supports delivery but may not scale effectively across multiple centres <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The twice weekly sessions over six weeks provide structured learning but may reduce feasibility for those with limited time or inconsistent schedules <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Small group sizes increase personalised support but limit overall programme capacity and may not meet demand <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Accessibility supports such as large print handouts and hearing loops improve feasibility for learners with disabilities but require ongoing resource commitments <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Track based learning covering basics, everyday services, and safety offers an innovative structure tailored to different needs <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Including a skills checklist and confidence survey introduces innovative monitoring and provides richer data than attendance alone <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Emphasising scam recognition addresses key fears expressed in feedback and strengthens relevance and innovation <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* The programme combines practical tasks with safety education, which may increase its overall perceived value and long term sustainability <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>8 marks</marks> for balanced evaluation that considers feasibility and innovation with reasoned judgement.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Responses must evaluate rather than simply describe features. Stronger responses will comment on both strengths and limitations relating to feasibility and innovation.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
The intervention is feasible because it uses a clear staffing model, although depending on rotating specialists may make scaling difficult. The twice weekly structure offers continuity but may be challenging for older adults with irregular commitments. Small groups enhance learning quality but constrain total capacity. Accessibility supports improve participation but require sustained investment.  

  
The programme is innovative because its track based design tailors learning to individual needs. The inclusion of skills checklists and confidence surveys provides more meaningful outcome data. Emphasising scam recognition reflects participant concerns and increases the programme’s relevance. Overall, the intervention balances feasible delivery with meaningful innovation.

parts.content: With reference to the programme and your own inquiries, recommend an intervention for the next 12 months which will maximise confidence in the programme and accessibility
parts.markscheme:
**Recommend (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**

  
**Pipeline stages / Key points / Answer points**

  
* Introducing mobile or outreach sessions such as home visits or pop up events can support participation among individuals who cannot travel easily <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Providing transport support or coordinated shuttles increases accessibility for those with mobility or distance barriers <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Offering structured practice with authentication processes such as two factor codes can reduce panic and increase confidence <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Implementing slower paced or repeated login rehearsals supports learners who need additional time <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Incorporating peer support models or private help options can reduce feelings of shame during learning <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Training staff in trauma informed methods can improve emotional safety for older adults <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Expanding scam recognition services such as drop in message checks or a support hotline directly addresses participant fears <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Developing partnerships with banks or health providers can strengthen fraud education and increase trust <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Monitoring meaningful outcomes such as successful task completion provides better insight into progress than attendance <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Using the skills checklist over the twelve month period supports ongoing tracking of confidence and competence <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Offering follow up sessions ensures learners retain skills and maintain confidence <marks>1 mark</marks>  
* Communicating programme improvements clearly to participants and caregivers can build trust in the programme <marks>1 mark</marks>  

**Awarding marks**

  
Award up to <marks>12 marks</marks> for a well reasoned recommendation that integrates source material and appropriate own knowledge.  

**Marking guidance**

  
<note>Answers should present a coherent twelve month plan that maximises confidence and accessibility. Responses that only propose a list of actions without justification should be limited. Students must link recommendations to identified needs or challenges.</note>  

**Sample answer**

  
A twelve month intervention could begin by introducing outreach sessions and home visit options to support people who cannot travel. Transport assistance would also help those with mobility challenges. To build digital confidence, the programme could offer practice sessions for authentication steps, including slower timed login rehearsals. Creating private support spaces and using trauma informed teaching would reduce feelings of shame.  

  
Expanding scam awareness through a hotline and drop in support would address safety concerns. The programme could partner with banks and health organisations to strengthen fraud education. Progress should be measured through task completion and skills checklists rather than attendance alone. Finally, regular follow up sessions and clear communication of improvements would help maintain confidence and accessibility over the full twelve months.
